chunks.text: Alexei (Alyosha) Efros is a Professor at UC Berkeley who joined in 2013. Previously, he spent a decade on the faculty at Carnegie Mellon University and has been affiliated with École Normale Supérieure/INRIA and University of Oxford. His research focuses on computer vision and computer graphics, particularly at their intersection, using data-driven techniques to solve problems with large amounts of unlabeled visual data. Efros earned his PhD in 2003 from UC Berkeley and holds a BS from the University of Utah in 1997.

Efros has received numerous awards including the CVPR Best Paper Award (2006), Sloan Fellowship (2008), Guggenheim Fellowship (2008), Okawa Grant (2008), SIGGRAPH Significant New Researcher Award (2010), three PAMI Helmholtz Test-of-Time Prizes (1999, 2003, 2005), the ACM Prize in Computing (2016), Diane McEntyre Award for Excellence in Teaching Computer Science (2019), Jim and Donna Gray Award for Excellence in Undergraduate Teaching of Computer Science (2023), and PAMI Thomas S. Huang Memorial Prize (2023).

His research areas include Artificial Intelligence (AI), Graphics (GR), and Computer Vision. He is associated with the Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR). Efros teaches CS C280: Computer Vision, scheduled TuTh 15:30-16:59 in Hearst Mining 390.

Selected publications include 'Unpaired image-to-image translation using cycle-consistent adversarial networks' (2017), 'Curiosity-driven Exploration by Self-supervised Prediction' (2017), 'Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning by Context Prediction' (2015), 'What makes Paris look like Paris?' (2012), 'Ensemble of Exemplar-SVMs for Object Detection and Beyond' (2011), 'Unbiased look at dataset bias' (2011), 'Beyond categories: The visual memex model for reasoning about object relationships' (2009), 'Scene completion using millions of photographs' (2007), 'Recovering surface layout from an image' (2007), and 'Texture synthesis by non-parametric sampling' (1999).

Selected honors and awards include the Jim and Donna Gray Award for Excellence in Undergraduate Teaching of Computer Science (2023), PAMI Thomas S. Huang Memorial Prize (2023), Diane S. McEntyre Award for Excellence in Teaching Computer Science (2019), PAMI ICCV Helmholtz Prize (2017), ACM Prize in Computing (2016), PAMI ICCV Helmholtz Prize (2013), SIGGRAPH Significant New Researcher Award (2010), NSF CAREER Award (2010), Okawa Research Grant (2008), Sloan Research Fellow (2008), Guggenheim Fellow (2008), NSF CAREER Award (2006), and CVPR Best Paper Award (2006).
